chunks.title: TEXTBOOK
chunks.content:
Chaffin, Andersson, & Martin, “Occupational Biomechanics” (4th Edition), 2006, John Wiley & Sons.
*The 3rd Edition is an acceptable substitute. Note the page number difference for reading assignment.

chunks.title: COURSE OBJECTIVES
chunks.content:
• Provide an understanding of the mechanical behavior of the human musculoskeletal system and component tissues when physical work is performed
• Teach fundamental biomechanical concepts, principles, tools, and methods for the measurement of human physical attributes, evaluation of human mechanical capabilities, and modeling of the human musculoskeletal system (or components thereof)
• Illustrate how concepts, principles, data, and models can be applied to improve human physical performances as well as the recognition and control of the risk of musculoskeletal disorders during work or daily living activities

chunks.title: PREREQUISITES
chunks.content: ISEN 330 Human Systems Interaction (or ISEN 630 or equivalent)

chunks.title: GRADING
chunks.content:
• Homework #1-Group Project: 10%
• Homework #2-Individual Project: 8%
• Homework #3-Group Project: 10%
• Homework #4-Final Group Project: 15% for undergraduate; 10% for graduate students
• Exam 1: 25%
• Exam 2: 30%
• Miscellaneous (attendance, steady improvement, etc.): 2%
**Suggested group size: 2-4 for graduate students; 3-5 for undergraduate students. Graduate students are also required to conduct and write a literature review on a selected topic, which accounts for 5%
The final letter grade is determined from the weighted total according to the following scale:

| Graduate | A 90-100 | B 75-89.9 | C 60-74.9 | D 48-59.9 | F 0-47.9 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Undergrad | A 85-100 | B 72-84.9 | C 60-71.9 | D 48-59.9 | F 0-47.9 |

***Curving may be implemented at the discretion of the instructor.

chunks.title: ACADEMIC INTEGRITY STATEMENT (Course Specific Enforcement)
chunks.content:
I, Xudong Zhang, as the rest of the Industrial & Systems Engineering Faculty, uphold the Aggie Honor Code as an axiom of our academic excellence. We consider its sincere observance to be essential for membership in our department and Texas A&M. We extend you the trust conferred to those who faithfully adhere to our honor code. Abuse of this trust is intolerable, thus I will report and assign an extreme penalty to those who do not stand with us in preserving the integrity symbolized by the Aggie Honor Code, “An Aggie does not lie, cheat, or steal or tolerate those who do

In this course, the penalty for any violation of the Aggie Honor Code, as minimal as it may be, is F*.

chunks.title: COURSE CONDUCT
chunks.content:
• Homework projects are due at the beginning of the class. There will be a 20-point per day deduction for late projects (any portion of a day will be counted as one day).
• If you are unable to complete work or attend examination in time due to medical, family, or religious reasons, notify the instructor well before (>24 hours) the critical time, and be prepared to provide verification of the reasons and make up the work promptly.
• Be professional and honest in completing assignments and tests. Take pride in your own work: it represents your understanding of the class material and synthesizes what you have learned. As such, it should be your own words and style.
• Grade disputes must be returned in writing within two days (48 hours) from the time you receive the corrected exam or homework assignment. The instructor or TA/grader reserves the right to re-examine and re-grade the entire work when reviewing the dispute.
• Participation in the class and evidence of steady improvement will be considered favorably in determining the final grade, particularly when the score is on the threshold of a higher or lower grade. Class attendance will be checked randomly a few times during the semester.
